title: Decomposition theorem for semisimple algebraic holonomic D-modules
speaker: Takuro Mochizuki
abstract: Decomposition theorem for perverse sheaves on algebraic varieties, proved by Beilinson-Bernstein-Deligne-Gabber, is one of the most important and useful theorems in the contemporary mathematics. By the Riemann-Hilbert correspondence, we may regard it as a theorem for regular holonomic D-modules of geometric origin. Rather recently, it was generalized to the context of semisimple holonomic D-modules which are not necessarily regular.

proof_stages.label: Holonomic D-modules and functorial calculus
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines D_X-modules, characteristic varieties and holonomicity, then recalls proper pushforward and the six-operation formalism for algebraic holonomic D-modules.

proof_stages.label: Regular Riemann-Hilbert background and classical decomposition
proof_stages.summary: The de Rham functor, constructibility, regular holonomic D-modules and the Riemann-Hilbert correspondence are recalled in order to state the BBDG decomposition theorem for semisimple geometric-origin regular objects and its Hodge-theoretic refinements.

proof_stages.label: General semisimple holonomic theorem and enhancement strategy
proof_stages.summary: The target theorem is stated for arbitrary semisimple algebraic holonomic D-modules under projective pushforward, and the proof strategy is reduced to adding a suitable twistor-type structure and characterizing semisimplicity by the existence of that structure.

proof_stages.label: Twistor structures and harmonic bundles as Hodge replacements
proof_stages.summary: Simpson's twistor structures and metatheorem are introduced as the analogue of Hodge structures, and polarized variations of pure twistor structure are related to harmonic bundles through the λ-flat family degenerating to Higgs bundles.

proof_stages.label: Smooth projective case as the guiding mechanism
proof_stages.summary: For smooth projective morphisms and flat bundles, Simpson's functoriality and hard Lefschetz for harmonic bundles together with the Corlette-Simpson correspondence give the decomposition and semisimplicity statement in the nonsingular case.

proof_stages.label: Twistor D-modules and projective hard Lefschetz
proof_stages.summary: The Sabbah-Mochizuki theory of pure and mixed twistor D-modules is summarized, including six operations, projective pushforward of polarizable pure objects, and hard Lefschetz for the underlying holonomic D-modules.

proof_stages.label: Wild harmonic and irregular singularity analysis
proof_stages.summary: To handle singular and irregular objects, the talk introduces tame and wild harmonic bundles, their meromorphic prolongations, Stokes and KMS filtrations, reductions to graded/limit objects, and birational resolution of turning points.

proof_stages.label: Semisimplicity correspondence and final assembly
proof_stages.summary: The analysis yields a correspondence between semisimple algebraic holonomic D-modules and polarizable pure twistor D-modules of the required type, so functoriality and hard Lefschetz for twistor D-modules imply the decomposition theorem.

examples.name: Quotient by a single differential operator
examples.note: The module D_X/D_XP is used to show concretely how a linear differential equation P(u)=0 is encoded by a D-module and recovered by morphisms to O_X.
examples.engagement_reason: He forms the quotient by a left ideal generated by an operator and identifies morphisms from it to O_X with holomorphic solutions of the corresponding differential equation.

examples.name: Flat bundles and the structure sheaf
examples.note: A holomorphic vector bundle with integrable connection, in particular O_X, illustrates both the basic notion of a D-module and the holonomic condition via the zero section characteristic variety.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains how an integrable connection gives a D_X-action and checks holonomicity by identifying the structure sheaf’s characteristic variety as the zero section.

examples.name: D_X as a non-holonomic D-module
examples.note: The sheaf D_X is first given as the most basic module over itself and later used as a contrast because its characteristic variety is all of T^*X, so it is not holonomic unless dim X=0.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies the characteristic variety of D_X with the full cotangent bundle and uses the dimension criterion to conclude it is generally non-holonomic.

passes.reason: D_X is used as a basic D-module that fails to be holonomic when dim X>0.

examples.name: Ordinary differential equation with singularities at 0 and 1
examples.note: An ODE whose singular points are z=0 and z=1 is used to display the characteristic variety as the zero section plus cotangent fibers and hence to make holonomicity visible in dimension one.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the ODE example by locating singularities at 0 and 1 and describing the corresponding characteristic variety to verify holonomicity.

examples.name: Higgs equation on a one-dimensional base
examples.note: The curve case is mentioned to make the harmonic metric condition concrete, since then the full integrability condition is equivalent to the usual Higgs equation.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes the harmonic-bundle curvature condition and explains why, on a one-dimensional base, the type decomposition reduces it to the Higgs equation.

examples.name: Rank-one wild harmonic bundle from a meromorphic function
examples.note: A meromorphic function f gives a trivial line bundle with Higgs field df and trivial metric, illustrating how irregular meromorphic behavior appears as a wild harmonic bundle.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly constructs the rank-one wild harmonic bundle from a meromorphic function as the trivial bundle with Higgs field df and trivial metric.

examples.name: Polarized variations of complex Hodge structure as tame harmonic bundles
examples.note: Polarized variations of Hodge structure are cited as tame harmonic bundles because their Higgs fields are nilpotent, connecting the twistor setting back to classical Hodge theory.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses polarized variations of complex Hodge structure to produce tame harmonic bundles and explains the reason via nilpotence of the Higgs field, later discussing their extensions and limiting mixed Hodge structures.
